ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Fish, grilled) used as closest defensible parent match; no specific code exists for Indian-spiced tandoor-grilled fish tikka.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific preparation; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 180 g (1 plate of 4–6 pieces), providing roughly 320 kcal, 35 g protein, and 18 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, tilapia, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175177), adapted with marinade and cooking-loss adjustments
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for ajwaini machli tikka exists in HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. USDA FDC tilapia (dry-heat cooked, FDC ID 175177) was used as the closest approved-source analogue for the fish base. Marinade contributions (yogurt, spices, oil) were estimated using USDA FDC component entries for plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 170903) and common Indian spices. Input per_100g values from the GrabFood-sourced record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA-derived estimate and found broadly consistent; values retained with medium confidence. Energy cross-check: 19.44 g protein × 4 + 10.0 g fat × 9 + 2.78 g carb × 4 = 77.76 + 90.0 + 11.12 = 178.88 kcal, consistent with 177.78 kcal reported. ICMR-NIN source listed in input supports menu listing only and was not used as primary nutrient evidence.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — consulted; no direct entry for fish tikka; GI not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ajwaini Machli Tikka contains only 2.78 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which 0.56 g is sugar. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Veritas protocol. Glycemic load per serving estimated as (assumed GI ~50 × 2.78 g available carb per 100 g × 1.8 serving factor) / 100 = ~0.28 GL units — clinically negligible. University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no direct entry found for fish tikka preparations.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 361 mg per 100 g (approximately 650 mg per 180 g serving) approaches one-quarter of the 2,300 mg daily recommended limit in a single serving.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt marinade preparation and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as chutneys or pickles to keep total meal sodium within recommended limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.78 g per 100 g) and high protein (19.44 g per 100 g) produce a negligible glycaemic load, supporting stable postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ajwaini Machli Tikka is a suitable protein-rich, low-carbohydrate choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid sweetened sauces or high-GI accompaniments to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean white fish provides high-quality protein with moderate total fat (10 g per 100 g) and low saturated fat (2.78 g per 100 g); tandoor grilling without deep-frying minimises added fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is a heart-friendly protein source; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor sodium intake per serving and consider requesting fatty fish (e.g., salmon) for greater omega-3 benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) in the marinade may provide modest benefit; lean white fish avoids the arachidonic acid load of red meat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade-derived spices (chilli powder) should monitor for symptom flares; consider requesting salmon tikka for higher omega-3 anti-inflammatory content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ajwaini Machli Tikka is an Indian grilled fish dish in which boneless white fish fillets (commonly dory or basa) are marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture featuring ajwain (carom seeds) as the signature flavouring agent, alongside turmeric, red chilli powder, garam masala, ginger, garlic, lemon juice, and oil. The marinated fish is then cooked in a tandoor (traditional clay oven) at high heat, producing a dish with a lightly charred exterior, a moist and flaky interior, and a distinctive herbal-peppery aroma from the ajwain. Ajwain seeds are known in Ayurvedic tradition for their digestive and carminative properties. The dish is popular in Indian restaurants across Singapore and is typically served as a starter or main course with mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 178 kcal, 19.4 g protein, 10 g fat (2.8 g saturated), and only 2.8 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option. Sodium is moderate at 361 mg per 100 g. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-certified in most Singapore restaurant contexts. It is clinically suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycaemic load, and for cardiovascular health due to its lean protein profile and tandoor cooking method that avoids deep-frying.

Is fish tikka good for diabetics?

What spices are in fish tikka marinade?
What fish is used in fish tikka?
Is fish tikka high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for ajwaini machli tikka exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values were adapted from USDA FDC tilapia (dry-heat cooked, FDC ID 175177) with marinade component adjustments, and cross-validated against the input ai_generated values (GrabFood, confidence 0.7). Internal energy cross-check confirms macro consistency. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted source match and ai_generated input provenance.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific Indian-spiced tandoor-grilled fish preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero for lean white fish grilled without hydrogenated fats; consistent with USDA FDC tilapia data. 3. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values for the composite dish as prepared (with marinade and tandoor cooking) are not individually reported in the adapted USDA FDC source and were not available in HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN for this specific preparation. 4. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (2.78 g per 100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Veritas protocol for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. 5. Input source listed ICMR-NIN as supporting 'menu_listing' only — not used as primary nutrient source. USDA FDC tilapia (FDC ID 175177) used as closest approved-source analogue; this is an approved secondary global source per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 5.1. 6. Ethnic tags 'Naan' and 'Biryani' from input were not carried forward as they are dish names, not ethnic identifiers. 7. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (19.44), fat_g (10.0), and carbohydrate_g (2.78) are all non-zero. 8. foodex2_match_type corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'adapted' in prior iteration) to comply with allowed values: closest_parent, exact, none.
